title: Why OKRs Fail (And How to Fix Them)
author: Minds Connected
excerpt: Most companies adopt OKRs with the best intentions, but fall into the same traps. Here are the most common failure patterns — and what to do instead.
content:
Objectives and Key Results have become one of the most popular goal-setting frameworks in the world — used by Google, Spotify, and thousands of fast-growing companies. Yet the majority of OKR implementations quietly fail within the first year.

Here are the most common reasons why — and what you can do about it.

1. OKRs become a copy of the to-do list

The most frequent mistake we see is teams writing tasks as Key Results: "Launch new onboarding flow" or "Hire two engineers." These are outputs, not outcomes. A Key Result should measure change — a shift in user behaviour, revenue, retention, or quality.

Fix: Ask "How will we know this Objective was achieved?" The answer should be a number that moves.

2. They are set top-down without dialogue

When leadership drops OKRs on teams without conversation, people feel measured, not motivated. Alignment is not the same as compliance.

Fix: Use a cascade-and-negotiate model. Leadership sets direction, teams propose their own OKRs in response, and both sides align through dialogue.

3. No check-ins — or too many

OKRs that are reviewed once per quarter become irrelevant fast. But weekly status meetings that feel like interrogations kill psychological safety. Neither extreme works.

Fix: A lightweight fortnightly check-in (15 minutes, confidence scores only) keeps OKRs alive without creating bureaucracy.

4. 100% achievement is celebrated

If your team hits 100% of their OKRs every quarter, your OKRs are too safe. The framework is designed for ambitious thinking — a 70% achievement rate on a stretch goal is often better than a 100% on a comfortable one.

Fix: Separate committed OKRs (must hit) from aspirational OKRs (meant to stretch). Calibrate expectations accordingly.

The bottom line

OKRs are a communication tool, not a performance management system. When they work, they create clarity, focus, and energy. When they fail, it is almost always a people and culture problem — not a framework problem.

If your OKR rollout has stalled, get in touch — we have helped organisations across Europe reset and rebuild their OKR practice from the ground up.

title: Agile Is Not a Methodology — And That Misunderstanding Is Costing You
author: Minds Connected
excerpt: Most organisations adopt Scrum or SAFe and call themselves Agile. But Agile is a mindset, not a process. Here is why that distinction matters more than you think.
content:
Every week we speak with leaders who say their organisation "does Agile." They run sprints. They have standups. They use Jira. And yet, something is not working — teams feel like they are just going through the motions, and the promised speed and adaptability never quite materialise.

The root cause is almost always the same: Agile was adopted as a process, not as a mindset.

What the Agile Manifesto actually says

The original Agile Manifesto — written in 2001 by 17 software practitioners — is twelve principles and four values. It says nothing about two-week sprints, story points, or velocity charts. Those came later, from frameworks like Scrum and Kanban that were built on top of the mindset.

The values are simple but radical:

  • Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
  • Working software over comprehensive documentation
  • Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
  • Responding to change over following a plan

Notice the emphasis. It is not "ignore processes" or "skip documentation." It is about where you put your energy when trade-offs arise.

Why process-first Agile fails

When organisations adopt the ceremonies without the values, you get what the community calls zombie Scrum — the rituals exist, but there is no heartbeat. Teams do retrospectives but nothing changes. Product owners exist but have no authority. Sprints end but nothing ships.

The framework becomes a new form of bureaucracy, just with different vocabulary.

What to do instead

Start with the problem, not the framework. Ask your team: What is slowing us down? What information are we missing? Who do we not talk to enough? Then look for practices that address those specific problems.

Agile works best as a continuous experiment — try something, measure it, adjust. That loop itself is Agile thinking.

If your transformation has stalled or you are not sure where to start, we would love to help. Real change is possible — it just requires more than installing a new process.
